items.myth: Decision trees make feature creation unnecessary.
items.reality: Trees can split on individual columns and approximate interactions, but explicit domain features (ratios, recency, flags) still improve interpretability, reduce depth, and help linear baselines that trees are often compared against.
items.example: A tree may eventually split on price and sqft separately; price_per_sqft gives one interpretable node aligned with how appraisers think.

items.myth: More features always improve the model.
items.reality: Each new column adds noise risk and maintenance cost; redundant or leaky features hurt generalization. Creation should be purposeful, not exhaustive.
items.example: Adding both price, sqft, and price_per_sqft without regularization can inflate variance for linear regression while adding little new signal.

items.myth: Datetime features are just extracting year, month, and day.
items.reality: Effective datetime engineering includes elapsed durations, business calendars (holidays), cyclical encodings, and timezone normalization—not only calendar parts.
items.example: days_since_last_purchase often beats month alone for churn; hour sin/cos beats raw hour for models sensitive to numeric distance.

items.myth: Feature creation and feature extraction are the same thing.
items.reality: Creation uses fixed formulas you define; extraction learns parameters (vocabulary, encodings) from training data via fit/transform. They solve different problems and compose in sequence.
items.example: price_per_sqft is created with division; TF–IDF from review text is extracted by TfidfVectorizer.fit on training reviews.
